summary: Joel Spolsky rounds up reader and industry reactions to his earlier post about improving web browsers. The post collects links from W3C representatives, developers, and critics who respond to Joel's call for better browser capabilities. Reactions range from enthusiastic agreement (with W3C's Dean Jackson suggesting improvements may come soon) to alternative proposals like Microsoft's ClickOnce deployment technology. The post serves as a follow-up showing the conversation Joel's browser critique sparked across the web development community.
key_points: W3C's Dean Jackson signaled that major browser vendors were ready to address Joel's concerns about web application capabilities
Multiple developers shared their frustrations with browser limitations, including JavaScript security restrictions and lack of right-click menu access
One developer noted that building a web app equivalent to a Win32 application took two years instead of six months due to browser limitations
A dissenting voice argued that deployment technologies like Microsoft's ClickOnce and Java Web Start would solve the problem better than browser improvements
money_quotes: Joel may get some of his wishes sooner than he thinks (let's hope!)
The current Javascript security philosophy can be easily summarised thus: 'No.'
The process took me two years. I think I could have done it in six months if I had had the following…
I want a way to access a browser's right-click menu.
What's that, too platform independent? Java will come with something similar, in fact Java Web Start apps are pretty much already there.
key_insight: Joel's earlier call for better browser capabilities resonated widely, drawing responses from W3C officials and developers alike, revealing deep frustration with the gap between native and web application capabilities in 2004.
